children.name: Cephalopyrus
children.description: A monotypic genus for the Fire-capped Tit, a small greenish-yellow tit of the Himalayan foothills with a brilliant orange-red crown patch. It was once placed in the penduline tit family Remizidae but molecular studies place it firmly within Paridae.

children.name: Cephalopyrus flammiceps
children.description: A small, greenish-yellow tit of open forests and scrub in the Himalayas, from Pakistan to Myanmar. The male has a striking orange-red crown patch during the breeding season. It feeds actively in the canopy on insects and nectar.

children.name: Parus
children.description: The great tits — large, bold members of the family with striking black-and-yellow plumage and a versatile, opportunistic diet. The Great Tit is one of the most studied birds in the world, a model organism for research into ecology, behaviour, and the evolutionary consequences of urbanisation. Its two-note song is famously variable and can be rendered in dozens of dialects across its range.

children.name: Parus major
children.description: The great tit is a small passerine bird in the tit family Paridae. It is widespread and common throughout Europe, the Middle East, Central Asia and as far east as the Amur River. It also occurs in parts of North Africa where it is generally resident in any sort of woodland.

children.name: Parus minor
children.description: Parus minor (Japanese Tit) is a great tits species found in Asia region.

children.name: Parus monticolus
children.description: Parus monticolus (Green-backed Tit) is a great tits species found in Asia region.

children.name: Parus cinereus
children.description: Split from the Great Tit complex, this species ranges from Iran through South and Southeast Asia to Indonesia. Its grey back and white cheek patch distinguish it from the yellow-vented northern forms.

children.name: Parus bokharensis
children.description: A pale, desert-adapted tit of Central Asian river valleys and oases, sometimes considered a subspecies of the Great Tit. It has whitish underparts and a grey back with little yellow.

children.name: Cyanistes
children.description: The blue tits — small, acrobatic tits with vivid blue-and-yellow plumage. The Eurasian Blue Tit (Blåmes) is one of the most familiar garden birds across Europe, famous for its ability to hang upside-down on the outermost branches to reach insects, and for its habit of opening milk bottle tops — a learned behaviour that spread across Britain in the 20th century.

children.name: Cyanistes caeruleus
children.description: The Eurasian blue tit is a small passerine bird in the tit family, Paridae. It is easily recognised by its blue and yellow plumage and small size.

children.name: Cyanistes cyanus
children.description: Cyanistes cyanus (Azure Tit) is a blue tits species found in Asia region.

children.name: Cyanistes teneriffae
children.description: Cyanistes teneriffae (African Blue Tit) is a blue tits species found in Africa and Europe regions.

children.name: Periparus
children.description: Small, dark tits of coniferous forests, distinguished by their glossy black cap and bib. The Coal Tit is the most widespread, a diminutive bird that hoards food in winter — tiny caches of seeds and insects secreted in bark crevices, which it can remember and retrieve weeks later.

children.name: Periparus ater
children.description: Periparus ater (Coal Tit) is a coal tits species found in Europe and Asia and Africa regions.

children.name: Periparus rubidiventris
children.description: Periparus rubidiventris (Rufous-vented Tit) is a coal tits species found in Asia region.

children.name: Periparus melanolophus
children.description: Periparus melanolophus (Spot-winged Tit) is a coal tits species found in Asia region.

children.name: Periparus rufonuchalis
children.description: A dark tit of western Himalayan coniferous forests, with a rufous patch on the nape and a black bib extending to the belly. It is a vocal and active forager in the canopy, often at elevations above 2000 m.

children.name: Pardaliparus
children.description: Small, brightly coloured tits of East and Southeast Asian forests, formerly included in Periparus. The Yellow-bellied Tit is one of the most beautiful members of the family, resembling a living jewel with its black-and-yellow plumage and white crescent on the nape.

children.name: Pardaliparus venustulus
children.description: A gorgeous little tit of Chinese forests, the male with black cap, yellow underparts, and a white crescent on the nape. It is a high-altitude specialist that moves to lower elevations in winter.

children.name: Pardaliparus elegans
children.description: A Philippine endemic with a black cap, white cheeks, and yellow breast, variable across many islands with distinct subspecies showing differences in the extent of yellow and the patterning of the face.

children.name: Pardaliparus amabilis
children.description: Endemic to Palawan and nearby islands in the Philippines, this stunning tit has a black hood, deep yellow breast, and a white patch on the wing. It is a canopy forager in lowland and montane forests.

children.name: Poecile
children.description: Marsh and willow tits of the northern Palearctic — drab but charming birds in brown and white, best told apart by their calls. The Willow Tit excavates its own nest hole in rotten wood, while the Marsh Tit takes over existing cavities. The Siberian Tit is one of the hardiest passerines on Earth, surviving Arctic winters through shivering, food caching, and a remarkably high metabolic rate. In North America the genus includes the chickadees, among the most familiar and beloved backyard birds on the continent.

children.name: Poecile palustris
children.description: The marsh tit is a Eurasian passerine bird in the tit family Paridae and genus Poecile, closely related to the willow tit, Père David's and Songar tits. It is a small bird, around 12 cm (4.7 in) long and weighing 12 g (0.42 oz), with a black crown and nape, pale cheeks, brown back and greyish-brown wings and tail. Between 8 and 11 subspecies are recognised.

children.name: Poecile montanus
children.description: The willow tit is a passerine bird in the tit family, Paridae. It is a widespread and common resident breeder throughout temperate and subarctic Europe and across the Palearctic. The plumage is grey-brown and off-white with a black cap and bib.

children.name: Poecile lugubris
children.description: Poecile lugubris (Sombre Tit) is a marsh tits species found in Europe and Asia regions.

children.name: Poecile cinctus
children.description: Poecile cinctus (Siberian Tit) is a marsh tits species found in Europe and Asia regions.

children.name: Poecile davidi
children.description: Poecile davidi (Père David's Tit) is a marsh tits species found in Asia region.

children.name: Poecile atricapillus
children.description: The familiar chickadee of northern North America, named for its 'chick-a-dee' call whose number of terminal notes encodes the level of threat.

children.name: Poecile carolinensis
children.description: The southeastern counterpart of the Black-capped Chickadee, with which it hybridises along a narrow contact zone that is shifting north with warming winters.

children.name: Poecile gambeli
children.description: A chickadee of western coniferous mountains, distinguished by a white eyebrow stripe and known for exceptional spatial memory in recovering thousands of cached seeds.

children.name: Poecile rufescens
children.description: A richly coloured chickadee of the humid Pacific coast forests, with bright chestnut back and flanks.

children.name: Poecile hudsonicus
children.description: A brown-capped chickadee of the northern spruce forests, hardy enough to remain in the boreal zone through the depths of winter.

children.name: Poecile sclateri
children.description: A chickadee of pine and pine-oak forests from the southwestern US to Mexico, with a distinctive brownish cap and a white cheek patch. It is the southernmost member of the chickadee group.

children.name: Poecile hypermelaenus
children.description: A Central Chinese tit with an extensive black bib extending to the belly, a rich brown back, and white cheeks. It inhabits valleys and foothill forests.

children.name: Poecile weigoldicus
children.description: A poorly known tit of the mountains of Sichuan and nearby provinces in China, recently split from the Willow Tit complex. It has darker brown upperparts and a more extensive black bib.

children.name: Poecile superciliosus
children.description: A striking tit of the eastern Tibetan Plateau and central Chinese mountains, with a bold white supercilium, black cap, and rich chestnut-brown back. It favours scrub and conifer stands at elevations above 3000 m.

children.name: Poecile songarus
children.description: A Central Asian tit of the Tien Shan and Altai Mountains, with a brown cap and black bib, inhabiting mountain coniferous forests and juniper scrub.

children.name: Sittiparus
children.description: Varied tits of East Asia, recently split from Poecile based on genetic and morphological differences. These are colourful, pattern-faced tits restricted to islands and coastal regions of Japan, Taiwan, and eastern China.

children.name: Sittiparus varius
children.description: A strikingly patterned tit of Japan, Korea, and Taiwan, with a black-and-white face, chestnut breast, and blue-grey back. It forages in mixed-species flocks and is a regular visitor to garden feeders in Japan.

children.name: Sittiparus ierardi
children.description: Endemic to the Izu Islands south of Tokyo, this tit differs from the Varied Tit in its darker belly and reduced white on the face. It is a restricted-range species vulnerable to introduced predators.

children.name: Sittiparus owstoni
children.description: A large, colourful tit endemic to the southern Japanese islands (Izu and Ryukyu archipelagos), with a rich chestnut belly, slate-grey back, and black-and-white head pattern.

children.name: Sittiparus castaneoventris
children.description: Endemic to Taiwan, this tit is distinguished by a chestnut belly and a white patch on the nape. It inhabits montane forests, often joining mixed-species foraging flocks.

children.name: Sylviparus
children.description: A monotypic genus for the Yellow-browed Tit, a tiny, olive-green leaf-gleaning tit of Asian forests with a distinct warbler-like appearance and habits.

children.name: Sylviparus modestus
children.description: A tiny olive-green tit with a distinct pale yellow eyebrow, foraging like a warbler in the canopy of broadleaf evergreen forests from the Himalayas to Southeast Asia. It is often overlooked but easily identified by its bright yellow eyebrow.

children.name: Lophophanes
children.description: Crested tits — recognisable at a glance by their spiky, black-and-white crest, unique among European tits. The Crested Tit is a specialist of mature pine forests and rarely strays far from them, making it one of the few European birds that cannot easily recolonise areas after deforestation. It too caches food extensively through winter.

children.name: Lophophanes cristatus
children.description: The crested tit or European crested tit is a passerine bird in the tit family Paridae. It is a widespread and common resident breeder in coniferous forests throughout central and northern Europe and in deciduous woodland in France and the Iberian Peninsula. In Great Britain, it is mainly restricted to the ancient pinewoods of Inverness and Strathspey in Scotland, and rarely strays far from its haunts.

children.name: Lophophanes dichrous
children.description: Lophophanes dichrous (Grey-crested Tit) is a crested tits species found in Asia region.

children.name: Machlolophus
children.description: Yellow-crested tits of South and East Asia, with striking sulphur-yellow crests and underparts. They inhabit broadleaf and mixed forests from the foothills of the Himalayas to the islands of Japan and Taiwan, filling a similar ecological niche to the great tits of the western Palearctic.

children.name: Machlolophus spilonotus
children.description: Machlolophus spilonotus (Yellow-cheeked Tit) is a crested tits species found in Asia region.

children.name: Machlolophus holsti
children.description: Machlolophus holsti (Yellow Tit) is a crested tits species found in Asia region.

children.name: Machlolophus xanthogenys
children.description: A striking yellow-crested tit of the Himalayan foothills, with a black eye-line extending from the bill and a brilliant yellow breast and crest.

children.name: Machlolophus aplonotus
children.description: A yellow-crested tit of peninsular Indian forests, very similar to the Himalayan Black-lored Tit but with a more extensive black bib and a slightly different vocal repertoire.

children.name: Machlolophus nuchalis
children.description: A rare and local tit of thorn scrub and dry acacia forests in northwestern India and Pakistan. It has a striking black cap, white nape, and yellow underparts. IUCN status: Vulnerable.

children.name: Melaniparus
children.description: African black tits — the continent's dominant tit group, replacing the Eurasian Parus in sub-Saharan Africa. Most species are predominantly black with white or yellow markings, and many show striking white wing patches. They are active, acrobatic foragers in acacia savanna, miombo woodland, and montane forest across the whole of sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Melaniparus niger
children.description: A glossy black tit with white wing patches, widespread across southern and East African savannas and woodlands. It is a conspicuous and vocal forager, often in small flocks.

children.name: Melaniparus carpi
children.description: A small black tit of dry miombo woodlands in southern Africa, with a white belly and white outer tail feathers. It was long considered a subspecies of the Southern Black Tit.

children.name: Melaniparus albiventris
children.description: A black-and-white tit of East African savanna woodlands, with a white belly, white wing-bar, and white tips to the tail feathers.

children.name: Melaniparus leuconotus
children.description: A glossy black tit with a distinctive white back patch, found in the highlands of Ethiopia and Eritrea. It inhabits juniper and Hagenia woodlands above 2000 m.

children.name: Melaniparus funereus
children.description: A dark, sooty-black tit of West and Central African lowland rainforests, lacking the white wing patches of its savanna relatives. It forages in the canopy, often in mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Melaniparus rufiventris
children.description: A tit of Central African savannas and woodlands, with a black head, grey back, and rich rufous belly. It is a conspicuous but local inhabitant of miombo and mopane woodlands.

children.name: Melaniparus pallidiventris
children.description: A tit of miombo woodlands in south-central Africa, with a black cap, grey back, and pale cinnamon breast.

children.name: Melaniparus fringillinus
children.description: A small, attractive tit of East African savannas, with a grey back, white cheeks, and a distinctive rusty-red throat patch. It is often found in acacia woodlands.

children.name: Melaniparus fasciiventer
children.description: A dark tit of Central African montane forests, with a black head and breast boldly streaked with white. It is restricted to high-elevation forests in the Albertine Rift mountains.

children.name: Melaniparus semilarvatus
children.description: A large, glossy black tit with a large white shoulder patch, endemic to the lowland forests of West Africa. It is a specialised forest interior species, scarce and local throughout its range.

children.name: Melaniparus griseiventris
children.description: A grey-bellied tit of southern African miombo woodlands, with a black head and white cheeks. It is common across its range and readily joins mixed-species foraging flocks.

children.name: Melaniparus cinerascens
children.description: A pale grey tit of dry savannas in southwestern Africa, with a black cap and bib. It is closely associated with Acacia and mopane woodlands.

children.name: Melaniparus afer
children.description: A tit of the South African Cape region, with grey upperparts, a black cap, and white underparts. It inhabits fynbos and coastal scrub, uniquely adapted to this fire-prone Mediterranean shrubland.

children.name: Melaniparus leucomelas
children.description: A striking black-and-white tit of East African highlands, with a prominent white wing-bar and white outer tail feathers. It is active and conspicuous in montane forest and juniper woodland.

children.name: Melaniparus guineensis
children.description: A West African savanna tit with a black head and body, whitish underparts, and distinctive pale yellow eyes. It inhabits dry open woodlands and gallery forests.

children.name: Pseudopodoces
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Ground Tit of the Tibetan Plateau — a remarkable bird long classified as a crow relative due to its terrestrial habits and decurved bill. Molecular analysis revealed it to be a highly modified parid that has adapted to life above the treeline, nesting in rodent burrows and feeding on invertebrates in the sparse alpine steppe.

children.name: Pseudopodoces humilis
children.description: Pseudopodoces humilis (Ground Tit) is a ground tit species found in Asia region.

children.name: Baeolophus
children.description: The titmice of North America — small grey crested tits that are bold, vocal visitors to woodlands and feeders across the United States and Mexico.

children.name: Baeolophus bicolor
children.description: A perky grey titmouse with a pointed crest and large dark eyes, common in eastern woodlands and expanding its range northward.

children.name: Baeolophus atricristatus
children.description: A south-central species of Texas and Mexico, told from the Tufted Titmouse by its jet-black crest; the two interbreed where their ranges meet.

children.name: Baeolophus inornatus
children.description: A plain brownish-grey titmouse of California's oak woodlands, mated pairs of which defend a territory together year-round.

children.name: Baeolophus wollweberi
children.description: A small titmouse of southwestern oak and pine canyons, distinctively patterned with black-and-white facial 'bridle' markings.

children.name: Baeolophus ridgwayi
children.description: A plain grey titmouse of the interior western US, closely associated with pinyon-juniper woodlands. It was split from the Oak Titmouse in 1997 based on differences in song and genetics.

children.name: Melanochlora
children.description: A monotypic genus for the Sultan Tit, by far the largest member of the family, a crested black-and-yellow giant of Southeast Asian forests.

children.name: Melanochlora sultanea
children.description: The largest of all tits, glossy black with brilliant yellow underparts and a long floppy yellow crest, ranging through subtropical and tropical forests of South and Southeast Asia.
